front: Define a pendant vertex in a tree
back: A pendant vertex (or leaf) is a vertex with degree 1 in a tree. It has only one neighbor and is a terminal vertex.

front: Define distance between two vertices in a tree
back: Distance between two vertices is the number of edges in the unique path connecting them. Since trees are acyclic, this path is unique.

front: Define the center of a tree
back: The center of a tree is a vertex (or vertices) that minimizes the maximum distance to all other vertices. It has minimum eccentricity.
Eccentricity of a vertex is the maximum distance from that vertex to any other vertex in the tree.

front: Define the radius of a tree
back: The radius of a tree is the minimum eccentricity among all vertices. It equals the minimum distance from a center to the farthest vertex.

front: Define the diameter of a tree
back: The diameter of a tree is the maximum distance between any two vertices. It equals the maximum eccentricity.

front: What is the relationship between radius and diameter
back: For any tree, radius ≤ diameter ≤ 2 × radius. The diameter is at most twice the radius.

front: How do you find the center(s) of a tree algorithmically
back: Iteratively remove all pendant vertices (leaves) from the tree. The remaining vertices after this process are the centers (1 or 2 vertices).

front: What is a leaf node in a tree
back: A leaf node (same as pendant vertex) is a vertex with degree 1. It is a terminal vertex with no children in rooted tree terminology.

front: How many centers can a tree have
back: A tree can have either 1 or 2 centers. A path with even number of vertices has 1 center; path with odd number has 2 centers.

front: What is the eccentricity of a center vertex
back: The eccentricity of a center vertex equals the radius of the tree. This is the minimum possible maximum distance.
A path graph Pn is a tree with n vertices where vertices can be arranged in a linear sequence with each vertex connected to its adjacent neighbors.
A spanning tree is a subgraph that is a tree and includes all vertices of the original graph. It has exactly (n-1) edges for n vertices.
In a tree, there is exactly one unique path between any two vertices. This makes distance well-defined and simplifies many algorithms.

front: What is a weighted distance in a tree
back: Weighted distance is the sum of weights of edges along the unique path between two vertices. Used in applications like network routing.
